title: practical english usage - Part 319
content:
A  structure with object + to be + complement is possible in a formal style, but it vocabulary •  466 finished ► first     and   at  first      388 I went to get the car from the garage, but they weren't/hadn't finished.

How soon will you be/have finished, dear?

same meaning as to have finished.

With personal subjects, to be finished is often used in an informal style with the Is the report finished yet?

Finished can be used as an adjective meaning 'ready'.

finish      and   end For eventually, ►    453.

I wish I was paid at the beginning of the week and not at the end.

A declarative sentence has a full stop at the end.

waiting or delay.

At the end simply refers to the position of something. There is no sense of 4 at  the    end In the end, Mother knows best.

In the end, you can't get fit without exercise.

Another use of in the end is to mean 'after we  have considered everything'.

The tax man will get you in the end.

I left in the middle of the film. Did they get married in the end?

went to Brighton.

We made eight different plans for the weekend, but in the end we In the end suggests that something happens after changes or uncertainty.

need to cut down administrative expenses. (     ... And at last we need to Firstly, we need to increase profits. Secondly,... Thirdly,... And lastly, we Note that lastly (introducing the last item in a series) is not the same as at last.

At last! Where have you been?

At last can be used as an exclamation. (Finally cannot be used in this way.) Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary •  467 fit and suit ►    no for    and   during ►    377 for,   as,   because        and   since He was sent to prison for stealing.

They punished the child for lying.

I'm angry with you for waking me up.

We are grateful to you for helping us out.

to explain the behaviour that caused it.

For.. .ing can also be used after a description of a positive or negative reaction, 3 causes     of  reactions We use altimeters to measure height above sea level.

the purpose of a thing.

When  the clause has a person as subject, an infinitive is often used to express An altimeter is used for measuring height above sea level.

Is that cake for eating or Just for looking at?

a thing - what it is used for - especially when the thing is the subject.

For can be used before the -ing form of a verb to express the 'purpose' of 2 the   purposes       of  things:    -ing    forms     and   infinitives not (     . . .forseeingProfessor-Taylof-.) I went to the college to see Professor Taylor.

not                                                   or (     ... for having a drink     for to have a drink) We stopped at the pub to have a drink.

a person's purpose (►   112).

For is not used before a verb in this sense. The  infinitive alone is used to express I went to the college for an interview with Professor Taylor.

We stopped at the pub for a drink.

when it is followed by a noun.

For can be used to talk about somebody's purpose in doing something, but only 1  people's      purposes:      / went      for    an   interview.

468 for:   purpose      and   cause Tuesday would suit me very well for a meeting.

Suit can also be used to say whether arrangements are convenient.

Do you think this style suits me?

Red and black are colours that suit me very well, (     ... colours that fit me Suit refers to style, colour, etc.

These shoes don't fit me - have you got a larger size?

nor too small.

Fit refers to size and shape: if your clothes fit you, they are neither too big These words do not mean exactly the same.

467 fit   and   suit vocabulary  •  469 for, since, in and from: time It was the worst storm for/in ten years.

I haven't seen him  for/in months.

This is normal in American English.

After negatives and superlatives, in can be used to talk about duration.

3 in   after    negatives       and   superlatives She'd been working there since 1988.

She'd been working therefor a long time, (     ... since a long time?) moment.

With a past perfect, for and since refer to duration up to a particular past It's been raining since the beginning of the month.
